steps.title: Recognize the measurement gap
steps.content: Grasby et al. calculated probability changes for brand extensions but never directly measured perceived category similarity—the key variable in their research question: “whether perceived category similarity predicts consumers' likelihood of purchasing brand extensions.” They need both measures to test the relationship.

steps.title: Match the study design to the gap
steps.content: Choice A supplies the missing measurement: households rate category similarity, and those ratings are then correlated with the probability changes already calculated. This directly tests whether similarity predicts purchase behavior. Choices B and D measure brand recognition rather than category similarity, and choice C measures similarity within a single category rather than between the two categories in an extended-brand pair. That matches choice A — A is correct.
